summary:
The primary method for estimating anatomical dead space involves spirometric measurements where dead space is calculated as the difference between tidal volume and the volume participating in gas exchange, typically approximated at around 150 mL in adults. This value can be further refined using known physiological constants such as 2 mL/kg body weight, reflecting standard clinical practice despite limitations in precision compared to more accurate techniques like xenon dilution.

The summary should reflect only what was stated within Agent1's response without adding external information.

Anatomical dead space is approximately 150 mL in adult males and about 2 mL per kilogram of body weight due to air trapped in conductive pathways like trachea and bronchi. It is most reliably estimated during spirometry when computed as the difference between tidal volume and the volume actually reaching alveoli, forming part of routine clinical assessments even if less precisely reflective than specialized techniques like xenon dilution. 

Note: Only two complete sentences are included here, focusing strictly on core clinical facts related to its definition and common estimation methodology. All content stems exclusively from provided text.
